what:
OpenFn aws-s3 adaptor. The adaptor lets OpenFn workflows read from and write to Amazon S3.

It exposes 3 operations that you can call from a workflow step, listed in the Operations tab. Vendor homepage: https://aws.amazon.com/s3/.
connect:
Amazon Web Services supports hmac for API access.

The credential schema asks for accessKeyId, secretAccessKey, sessionToken, region, endpoint, and bucket. Point the credential at the base URL that matches your environment: https://s3.amazonaws.com, https://s3.<region>.amazonaws.com. Auth reference: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/API/sig-v4-authenticating-requests.html.
when:
Reach for this adaptor when your workflow needs to read or write data in Amazon S3.

Triggers haven't been catalogued yet — drive the workflow from a cron schedule or an upstream webhook in the meantime. Amazon Web Services can push events to OpenFn via webhooks (docs: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/NotificationHowTo.html), so reactive workflows are a good fit.
wire:
A typical wiring pattern is trigger → fetch → transform → write.

Drive the workflow from a cron schedule or an upstream webhook trigger. Then chain operations from this adaptor's Operations tab to fetch or write data in Amazon Web Services. Pass values between steps through state.data — every operation reads from and writes to job state.

To move data between systems, follow this step with another adaptor (e.g. write the fetched records into a downstream FHIR, DHIS2, or database adaptor).
scale:
When running Amazon Web Services jobs at volume:

- Prefer Amazon Web Services webhooks over polling — they cut latency and rate-limit pressure.
- Batch requests where the API supports it and avoid fan-out loops over large collections.
- Make jobs idempotent by keying on stable external IDs so a replay doesn't create duplicates.
- Surface 4xx/5xx responses with the vendor request ID in your logs, and treat 429s as retries with backoff.

Adapter note: S3 is XML/REST under SigV4; most integrations use the AWS SDK rather than raw HTTP.
best:
- Store Amazon Web Services credentials in OpenFn's credential store; never inline keys or tokens in job code.
- Pin @openfn/language-aws-s3 to v1.0.2 in package.json so an upstream change doesn't break a production workflow silently.
- Keep each step small — one logical API call per operation makes failures easier to diagnose and retry.
- Add defensive checks before transforming inbound payloads — vendor responses change between versions.
- Log enough context (resource id, run id, vendor request id) so an incident can be traced end-to-end.
